```{=latex}
\ifdefined\ArchTwoReviewBuild
\mainmatter
\fi
```

# The Architecture 2.0 Moonshot {#sec-moonshot}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"The purpose of computing is insight, not numbers."*
>
> — Richard Hamming, *Numerical Methods for Scientists and Engineers* (1962)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Richard Hamming, a pioneer in computer science, argued that
computing should yield insight rather than raw data. The distinction matters
here. AI-assisted architecture should help architects ask better questions,
test alternatives, explain mechanisms, and defend decisions. Producing more
candidates is useful only when it improves that work.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This chapter introduces the Architecture 2.0 moonshot and the Lighthouse prompt used throughout the book. It follows the historical progression from device scaling to parallelism and specialization, then asks what an AI-assisted architecture process would have to combine. The aim is not push-button chip design. It is a process that helps an architect formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend an efficient system design with evidence.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What capabilities must an AI-assisted architecture process combine to turn
system intent into an efficient system design that an architect can defend
with evidence?
:::



For roughly fifty years, processor generations improved through a familiar
progression. Device scaling regularly delivered faster, cheaper, and more
energy-efficient transistors. Quantitative analysis gave architects a way to
compare the resulting design choices. When scaling slowed, microarchitecture,
parallelism, and eventually domain specialization provided more of the gains
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach; @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge].
The change is visible in fifty years of
frontier microprocessor data. Clock frequency and typical power flatten in the
mid-2000s, while transistor counts continue to rise and core counts turn upward
(@fig-microprocessor-trends).

```{python}
#| label: fig-microprocessor-trends
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Frontier microprocessor trends from 1971 to 2021.** Data from Karl Rupp's public microprocessor dataset are summarized as the highest value in each four-year interval [@Rupp2022MicroprocessorTrendData]. Clock frequency and typical power flatten in the mid-2000s as Dennard scaling ends, while transistor counts continue to rise and core counts turn upward [@DennardEtAl1974Scaling; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon]. The figure shows frontier values rather than every processor; the era boundaries are approximate [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge].
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale line chart of frontier microprocessor trends from 1971 to 2021 with five series. Transistors per chip rise from a few thousand to tens of billions by 2021. Single-thread performance rises steeply until the mid-2000s and then slows. Clock frequency plateaus near 3 to 5 GHz and typical power near 100 to 500 W after about 2005, while logical core counts climb from one to the hundreds. Dashed vertical lines mark the approximate end of Dennard scaling around 2005 and the specialization turn around 2016. A dotted boundary past 2025 marks AI-assisted compositional design as a prospective era rather than a measured trend."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style

apply_style()

# Fifty-year microprocessor trend data; per-series frontier points
# (per-4-year-bucket maxima) transcribed from the public dataset
# github.com/karlrupp/microprocessor-trend-data (50yrs/*.dat, CC-BY 4.0),
# which compiles the Horowitz/Labonte/Shacham/Olukotun/Hammond/Batten
# collection through 2010 plus Rupp's additions through 2021. Values are
# dataset-transcribed, not recomputed; fractional years as in the dataset;
# transistors in thousands, single-thread performance in SpecINT x 1000.
# Era-boundary annotations (~2005 Dennard end, ~2016 specialization turn)
# are approximate: [@DennardEtAl1974Scaling; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon;
# @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Accessed 2026-07-14.
# Receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter1-micro-trend.csv
series = [
    {
        "label": "Transistors\n(thousands)",
        "color": COLORS["purple"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 6.1), (1982.3, 136), (1985.9, 274), (1989.5, 1210),
                   (1992.7, 3110), (1998.9, 15300), (2002.9, 221000),
                   (2007.0, 583000), (2009.7, 2.31e6), (2014.8, 5.7e6),
                   (2018.5, 2.11e7), (2021.9, 5.82e7)],
        "label_y": 5.82e7,
    },
    {
        "label": "Single-thread perf\n(SpecINT x 1000)",
        "color": COLORS["blue"],
        "points": [(1988.7, 83.5), (1994.8, 599), (1998.9, 3550),
                   (2002.4, 9390), (2007.0, 35200), (2010.3, 42000),
                   (2014.8, 66000), (2017.6, 83600), (2021.3, 105000)],
        "label_y": 1.05e5,
    },
    {
        "label": "Frequency (MHz)",
        "color": COLORS["orange"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 2.02), (1982.3, 6.1), (1985.9, 16.1),
                   (1988.7, 40.3), (1994.7, 297), (1997.5, 538),
                   (2002.4, 2250), (2004.9, 3650), (2007.7, 4660),
                   (2012.7, 3700), (2016.7, 4000), (2020.9, 3200)],
        "label_y": 3.2e3,
    },
    {
        "label": "Typical power (W)",
        "color": COLORS["red"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 0.922), (1982.3, 3.02), (1986.2, 3.02),
                   (1988.7, 3.96), (1993.0, 35.2), (1998.9, 90.6),
                   (2001.7, 98.2), (2003.4, 155), (2010.4, 168),
                   (2013.4, 300), (2018.5, 250), (2021.9, 500)],
        "label_y": 7.5e2,
    },
    {
        "label": "Logical cores",
        "color": COLORS["green"],
        "points": [(1971.9, 1), (1979.6, 1), (1985.9, 1), (1988.7, 1),
                   (1992.4, 1), (1995.4, 1), (1999.2, 1), (2006.0, 8),
                   (2010.2, 16), (2013.4, 240), (2016.5, 272), (2021.9, 208)],
        "label_y": 1.1e2,
    },
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 3.6))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.075, right=0.775, top=0.90, bottom=0.225)

ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlim(1970, 2032.5)
ax.set_ylim(0.4, 3e9)

for x in (2005, 2016):
    ax.axvline(x, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.8,
               linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)

era_labels = [
    (1987.5, "Device-scaling era"),
    (2010.5, "Parallelism era"),
    (2019.6, "Specialization\nera"),
]
for x, text in era_labels:
    ax.text(x, 6.5e8, text, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=6.2,
            fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"])

# Prospective era: annotation only, no data points and no band fill, styled
# like the other era boundaries but dotted to mark a prospective argument.
ax.axvline(2025, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.8,
           linestyle=(0, (1.5, 2.5)), zorder=1)
ax.text(2028.9, 6.5e8, "AI-assisted\ncompositional\ndesign?", ha="center",
        va="center", fontsize=5.4, fontweight="bold",
        color=COLORS["workload_ink"], linespacing=1.2)
ax.text(2028.9, 2.5e7, "(prospective,\nnot measured\ndata)", ha="center",
        va="top", fontsize=5.4, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.25)

ax.text(2004.3, 4e7, "Dennard scaling\nends", ha="right", va="center",
        fontsize=5.6, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.15)
ax.text(2016.4, 1.6e6, "specialization\nturn", ha="left", va="center",
        fontsize=5.6, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.15)

for s in series:
    xs = [p[0] for p in s["points"]]
    ys = [p[1] for p in s["points"]]
    ax.plot(xs, ys, color=s["color"], linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter(xs, ys, s=13, color=s["color"], zorder=3)
    ax.text(1.015, s["label_y"], s["label"], ha="left", va="center",
            fontsize=6.0, fontweight="bold", color=s["color"],
            clip_on=False, linespacing=1.15,
            transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform())

ax.set_xticks([1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010, 2020])
ax.set_yticks([1, 1e2, 1e4, 1e6, 1e8])
ax.set_yticklabels([r"$10^0$", r"$10^2$", r"$10^4$", r"$10^6$", r"$10^8$"])
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

add_note_box(
    fig,
    "Four-year frontier maxima from Rupp's 50-year dataset; "
    "era boundaries are approximate.",
    xywh=(0.075, 0.03, 0.70, 0.075),
    fontsize=5.6,
)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

Specialization brought major advances, but it also multiplied the number of
architectures, software interfaces, and physical constraints that designers
must consider. The rise of machine learning accelerated this change. The
authors of Project Brainwave described the resulting spread of GPUs, FPGAs,
neural processors, and other specialized hardware as a "Cambrian explosion"
[@ChungEtAl2018Brainwave]. New dataflows, memory systems, and programming
interfaces appeared in quick succession [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Much
of the coordination among workloads, compilers, simulators, RTL, and physical
design still falls to architects.

Specialization also made architecture decisions more tightly coupled. A new
dataflow can change memory traffic, compiler mapping, verification effort, and
physical feasibility at once. As the number of alternatives and cross-layer
dependencies grows, architects must coordinate more models, tools, and
evidence before they can defend a choice. While this historical expansion of cross-layer relationships introduces the pressure, @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales explains the mechanisms by which workload change, software dependence, evaluation cost, and coordination pressure make design work harder to complete. This pressure does not make AI
necessary, but it explains why architects are exploring AI assistance.

::: {.callout-field-note title="Field Note: Multidisciplinary Design Optimization (MDO)"}
**Observation:** Hardware specialization tightly couples the stack. An AI cannot explore the design space effectively because optimizing one parameter (e.g., dataflow) cascades into breaking constraints across different, siloed simulation tools (e.g., thermal, synthesis, compilation).

**Context:** In Aerospace Engineering (e.g., NASA's BLISS framework), designing an aircraft faces the exact same coupled constraint problem across Aerodynamics, Structures, and Acoustics. Instead of building a single "magic" monolith simulator, MDO orchestrates fast, machine-learning *surrogate models* for broad exploration, and reserves the expensive, siloed physics simulators only for final verification.

**Lesson:** This proves to architects that AI should act as the orchestrator of existing tools, not a replacement for them.
:::

AI is now being applied to the design process that produced those accelerators,
including architectural design-space exploration and physical placement
[@WangEtAl2026AgentDSE; @MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].
In this material, *AI* is an umbrella term rather than the name of one model
family. An architecture project may combine several AI methods with simulators,
compilers, EDA tools, and checking tools. Those methods may generate mechanisms
or edit artifacts, predict behavior and risk, search design spaces, or choose
experiments. They may also critique assumptions, repair invalid artifacts, or
check results. Not every project needs all of these roles. One may need more
promising alternatives, while another may be limited by simulator time,
physical-design runs, or the effort required to understand conflicting results.
AI should improve the architecture work between a design prompt and a
defensible decision rather than merely increase the amount of output.

Imagine an architect entering the following design brief into a generative,
tool-using AI system. The prompt tests system intent, not whether a current model can complete the design.

> **Lighthouse prompt.** Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a Tensor Core accelerator block under a 3 W TDP target in a 3 nm-class LP mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives.

We call this the *Lighthouse prompt*. It is deliberately audacious. No public
result reviewed for this book establishes an end-to-end answer. Its scale
exposes the architecture work that a smaller generation task would hide and
gives the material a long-term target for judging partial progress.

This compact prompt is not yet enough to guide architecture work. An architect
must still define the workload and baseline, choose meaningful alternatives,
connect them to executable tools, and decide what evidence could support or
overturn a recommendation. AI may assist with each part. It may also produce
candidate designs, scripts, and summaries faster than architects and reviewers
can evaluate them or connect them to a decision.

The prompt does not imply one model, one invocation, or one uninterrupted
agent. A credible answer would combine several methods, representations,
software components, tools, experiments, and reviews. The object of study is
therefore the complete AI-assisted architecture process and the decision it
helps an architect make, not the apparent fluency of one generated response.

Suppose a method proposes a thousand accelerators. If architects cannot test why
they differ, explain why most should be rejected, or state what would overturn
the apparent winner, the extra proposals have not advanced the architecture
decision. A fast analytical model could still help by screening out designs that
exceed the thermal budget before they consume scarce simulator or
physical-design time. The value comes from reducing the expensive engineering
work, not from the proposal count itself.

We use the same Lighthouse prompt throughout this material. That lets later
chapters change the method or the part of the stack under study without quietly
changing the workload, constraints, or standard of evidence.




AI in computer architecture is not new.
Researchers have used it for branch predictors, prefetchers, surrogate models,
reinforcement-learning[^fn-rl-reinforcement-learning] controllers, design-space
exploration, and autotuning.
Chen, Penney, and Jiménez survey this work component by component in *AI for
Computer Architecture: Principles, Practice, and Prospects*
[@ChenPenneyJimenez2020AIArchitecture]. Predictive design-space exploration and
tensor-program autotuning are two examples
[@IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE; @ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM]. In most of this work,
learning operates inside an artifact or performs a specific job within
architecture work defined by people.

[^fn-rl-reinforcement-learning]: Reinforcement learning is a machine-learning method in which an agent learns to choose actions that maximize a cumulative reward signal from its environment, often used to navigate complex design spaces [@SuttonBarto2018ReinforcementLearning]. In an AI-assisted architecture study, it is one way to search. Its proposals still require the same evidence and checks as those from any other method.

Recent generative and tool-using systems are being tested on bounded parts of
that work, including proposing architecture configurations, invoking tools,
and revising a search from returned results [@WangEtAl2026AgentDSE]. These
systems can also generate text and scripts, but those artifacts are only parts
of the work that connects an architecture question to a decision. Our scope
includes both learned components inside a design and AI systems used to conduct
the work. In either case, the important question is how the method changes the
evidence behind an architectural conclusion.

Architecture work has always moved through repeated analysis and revision.
Architects frame a problem, choose abstractions, construct models or
simulators, select workloads, explore alternatives, evaluate results, reject
weak candidates, and revise assumptions. This work moves from intent and workload characterization to architecture decomposition, construction, evaluation, explanation, and decision. Consider an architect weighing a
larger L2 cache for a stated workload mix. The architect might use gem5, a
cycle-level architecture simulator, or FireSim, an FPGA-accelerated full-system
RTL simulation platform, to sweep cache sizes and replacement policies across
representative workloads [@BinkertEtAl2011Gem5;
@KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim]. They would compare miss behavior, execution time,
and energy estimates, then explain whether locality, contention, or latency
hiding caused the result. Simulators, compilers, profilers, synthesis tools,
spreadsheets, dashboards, and design reviews all mediate the judgment about
whether the evidence supports a cache change.

For generative and tool-using systems, it is clearer to describe each method by
the architecture job it performs than by its model family.
@fig-architecture-development-triad previews the three roles most often used to
produce and select candidates. Generation proposes or edits architecture
artifacts, prediction estimates behavior and risk, and optimization searches
tradeoffs under constraints. Three supporting roles challenge and check that
work. Critique exposes weak assumptions, repair corrects invalid artifacts, and
verification tests whether a result passes the declared checks.

![**Three method roles within one architecture study:** Generation proposes artifacts and candidates, prediction estimates behavior and risk, and optimization searches tradeoffs under constraints. The architect still needs evidence, a mechanism-level explanation, and judgment about the result.](images/F1b-architecture-development-triad){#fig-architecture-development-triad width="85%" fig-alt="Three-circle diagram showing generation, prediction, and optimization as interacting method jobs within a broader AI-assisted architecture study."}

These jobs need not occur in a fixed order. Generation can expose a missing
constraint. Prediction can show that the available evidence does not separate
two candidates. Optimization can allocate scarce simulator or EDA runs, while
critique and verification can disqualify the apparent winner. Repair changes
the artifact and may invalidate earlier measurements.

To evaluate any of these contributions, an architect must be able to see what
the system inspected and changed, how it obtained feedback, which observations
counted as evidence, and which checks could reject its result. The responsible
architect still sets the intent, chooses the abstraction and fidelity,
interprets the architectural mechanisms, decides what evidence is adequate,
and determines which decisions may be automated. The degree of
automation can vary, but the study should state which decisions AI may make and
which remain with people. Automating a step does not transfer
responsibility for the architectural conclusion.

AI-assisted architecture extends a long history of design automation, but it
begins from a broader statement of intent than earlier tools.

@fig-automation-ladder places this broader form of AI participation in the
history of design automation. The dates are approximate. Each stage accepts a
broader statement of intent and produces a larger artifact.

1. **1980s--1990s (Logic Synthesis):** Logic synthesis translated Boolean and RTL descriptions into gate-level structure under library, physical, and timing constraints [@DeMicheli1994SynthesisOptimization].
2. **1990s--2000s (High-Level Synthesis):** High-level synthesis generated RTL candidates from algorithmic descriptions and moved part of the tactical microarchitecture search into tools [@CoussyMorawiec2008HighLevelSynthesis].
3. **2000s--2010s (Domain-Specific Synthesis):** Building on deductive program synthesis from the 1970s, domain-specific synthesis generated executable code from specifications constrained by domain-specific languages [@GulwaniPolozovSingh2017ProgramSynthesis].
4. **2010s--2020s (Agile Hardware Generation):** Agile hardware generation used parameterized templates, such as Chisel and its FIRRTL intermediate representation, to create specialized SoC instances from reusable IP [@chipyard].
5. **Prospective direction (AI-Assisted Architecture):** AI may help connect workload intent, software contracts, architectural alternatives, executable tools, experiments, and physical constraints inside a scoped study. The research question is whether the result can become a finding or decision supported by evidence, even when much of the study remains under human control.

![**Four historical stages and one prospective direction.** The historical stages accept progressively broader statements of intent and produce larger artifacts. AI-assisted architecture is shown as a research direction toward a scoped study that yields a finding or decision supported by evidence, not as an inevitable next stage. Era spans are approximate and follow the literature cited above.](images/F2b-automation-ladder-timeline){#fig-automation-ladder width="85%" fig-alt="Staircase diagram with four historical design-automation stages and a prospective AI-assisted architecture direction. From bottom to top: logic synthesis, high-level synthesis, domain-specific synthesis, agile hardware generation, and AI-assisted architecture. A horizontal arrow marks approximate time order and a vertical arrow marks the widening unit of intent and artifact."}

The earlier stages remain essential. Logic synthesis, for example, became
dependable because static timing analysis and equivalence checking could expose
bad netlists. AI-assisted architecture draws on these established tools while
connecting decisions made across stages. The evidence must identify which tool
produced each result and which assumptions limit how that result can be used.

Reviewable evidence from iterative work should include the proposals
considered, the results obtained, the candidates rejected or revised, and the
decisions that followed. Different readers use this information for different
reasons.

- **Researcher:** Describe an AI-for-architecture paper by naming the
  architecture question, the role AI played, the artifact or finding produced,
  and the evidence and limits behind it.
- **Tool builder:** Ask whether a harness keeps the inputs, settings,
  results, and failures that another method or engineer would need.
- **Author or reviewer:** Ask what would reject the claim, why the measured
  mechanism supports it, and which decision it can justify.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter, you can expand a compact architecture prompt into a
well-scoped
AI-assisted project. That means you can:

- **Context:** Distinguish established uses of machine learning in architecture from broader AI participation across architecture work.
- **Formulate:** Define the Lighthouse prompt in terms of workload, baseline, alternatives, constraints, and non-goals.
- **Explore and implement:** Identify meaningful alternatives and the executable artifacts and tool paths needed to compare them.
- **Evaluate and explain:** Specify experiments and observations that could support or overturn the result, then explain the architectural mechanisms involved.
- **Defend:** State a recommendation, its evidence limits, and the conditions that would reverse it.
- **Compare efficiency:** Treat efficiency as a multidimensional architectural claim rather than a single score.
:::


## Architecture 2.0 and Existing Practice {#sec-from-architecture-10-to-architecture-20}

These review questions apply whether people orchestrate every step or AI
participates in part of the work. Architecture 1.0 is shorthand for familiar,
human-orchestrated design. The architect defines the problem, chooses models,
uses tools, interprets feedback, and decides what to build. The Architecture
2.0 vision retains that practice while allowing AI to take part in more of the
work [@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20], as summarized in @tbl-arch1-vs-arch2.

| **Feature** | **Architecture 1.0** | **Architecture 2.0** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Orchestration** | Fully human-driven | AI-assisted and mixed-initiative |
| **Exploration** | Manual sweeps over small parameter spaces | Generative models proposing novel architectures |
| **Tool Execution** | Scripts and manual toolchain invocation | Autonomous tool agents handling execution and repair |
| **Decision Making** | Solely human insight | AI rationale paired with human final judgment |

: **Architecture 1.0 vs Architecture 2.0:** Moving from manual orchestration to AI-assisted workflows while retaining human accountability. {#tbl-arch1-vs-arch2}

AI may help interpret an initial
request, characterize workload distributions, propose an architecture
decomposition, construct artifacts, select and run evaluations, diagnose results,
and prepare a recommendation. These are possible forms of assistance across
several systems and methods, not a claim that one public system performs the
complete workflow. This book organizes that participation into six activities:
formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend computer architecture
decisions. These are longitudinal learner capabilities, whereas intent,
workload characterization, decomposition, construction, evaluation, explanation,
and decision describe recognizable architecture work. They overlap, but they are
not competing stage taxonomies. Producing a plausible
artifact may help, but it does not complete that work.

As AI takes on more work, the study evidence must identify the inputs and
artifact versions available to the system, the actions it may take, the
feedback it received, and the candidates it rejected. The architect remains
responsible for the intent, constraints, abstraction, evidence standard,
interpretation, and final decision. This information lets another architect
determine what happened and what the result can support.

In an AI-assisted cache study, a method might propose cache sizes and replacement policies, a surrogate[^fn-ml-surrogate-model] could screen them, and a critic might flag invalid workload assumptions, but the architect still decides whether the evidence supports a cache change.
The contribution might be a cache design, an architectural finding, a reusable
method or dataset, or a decision supported by the available evidence.

[^fn-ml-surrogate-model]: A surrogate model is a cheap, data-driven stand-in for an expensive simulator. In a design project it lets a method screen many candidates quickly, but its score is only a proxy. A promising candidate still needs a higher-fidelity check before it can support a design decision.

@fig-design-loop-breaks shows the practical difference. Both sides iterate, and
both depend on architectural judgment. The AI-assisted side makes the inputs,
artifact versions, permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, rejected
candidates, and decision authority explicit enough for a system and its
reviewers to use.

![**AI participation makes design state explicit.** Both studies iterate, but AI participation requires the inputs, artifact versions, permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, rejected candidates, and decision authority to be represented explicitly.](images/F2a-design-loop-breaks){#fig-design-loop-breaks width="85%" fig-alt="Two side-by-side loop diagrams compare human-orchestrated architecture work, where experts carry much of the study state, with an AI-assisted project that represents inputs, actions, evidence, rejection, and decision authority explicitly."}

An AI system that calls a simulator, writes RTL, or summarizes papers still
depends on sound systems engineering. Architects need to identify the likely
bottleneck, account for memory movement and physical limits, compare tradeoffs
numerically, and decide what evidence justifies the next step.

When AI participates, it should also present a concise rationale that connects
its proposals, tool results, and rejected alternatives to its recommendation.
That rationale is not evidence by itself. @sec-feedback-verification-trust covers the criteria that make an observation relevant evidence for a bounded claim. A rationale gives the architect a structured
account to inspect for missing assumptions, contradictions, or conclusions
that the measurements do not support.

Safety engineering offers a useful precedent. Later decisions are checked
against the assumptions, hazards, and evidence on which they depended
[@Leveson2012EngineeringSaferWorld]. If a later
workload changes the locality behavior, the architect can see why the earlier
conclusion may no longer apply.

These practices make an individual project easier to review. The larger
question is whether they can support AI across a much greater share of
architecture design.

## The Lighthouse Expresses Intent, Not a Complete Specification

Engineers use *moonshot* for an ambitious goal that requires more than
incremental improvement. X, the Moonshot Factory, describes it as the
intersection of a large problem, a radical solution, and a technological
breakthrough that makes progress plausible [@XCompanyMoonshotBlueprint]. The
full solution may remain distant, but the target should be specific enough to
organize experiments and show which advances matter.

Computer architecture has comparable precedents. The Mead and Conway VLSI
methodology[^fn-vlsi-complexity] joined abstractions and design rules to tools,
fabrication access, and pedagogy [@MeadConway1980VLSI]. The reduced-instruction-set program
reshaped the hardware/software contract around quantitative evidence
[@PattersonDitzel1980RISC]. Both efforts produced more than a single design.
They changed the methods architects used and the artifacts they could share.

[^fn-vlsi-complexity]: Early-1980s frontier microprocessors contained about $10^4$ to $10^5$ transistors. By 2021, frontier chips contained tens of billions [@Rupp2022MicroprocessorTrendData]. VLSI design was already outgrowing transistor-level practice. Mead and Conway made higher-level abstractions, design rules, and fabrication access part of a method that could be taught and shared.

> **Architecture moonshot.** An architecture moonshot is a difficult,
> long-term target that existing tools and design practice cannot yet
> address end to end. It seeks a major improvement in how architects
> use AI to formulate problems, explore and implement alternatives, evaluate
> and explain results, and defend decisions. Advances in AI, data, or tools
> must make at least part of the target practical to investigate without
> implying that the whole problem has been solved.

@fig-moonshot-venn separates the three conditions in the definition. The grand
challenge is architecture work that current, ad hoc coordination cannot handle
well. The radical target is a rigorous AI-assisted practice that changes how
architects formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend
designs. The enabling breakthrough is the combination of AI, data, and tools
that makes part of that target practical to investigate now. None of the three
is sufficient alone. Together, they identify an important architecture problem,
a different way to approach it, and a practical place to begin testing that
approach even though the complete goal remains out of reach.

![**Three conditions for an architecture moonshot:** The target combines a major architecture challenge, a substantial change in AI-assisted design work, and an advance in AI, data, or tools that makes part of the problem practical to study.](images/F1c-moonshot-venn){#fig-moonshot-venn width="85%" fig-alt="Three-circle Venn diagram showing an architecture moonshot at the intersection of a grand challenge, a radical practice target, and an enabling breakthrough."}

Later chapters examine bounded parts of this target rather than claiming to deliver it whole.

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

The Lighthouse prompt, which asks for a low-power 64-bit RISC-V compute
subsystem for mobile XR, fits all three conditions. Today, architects coordinate
decisions across workloads, software, hardware, tools, and physical design. The
long-term aim is for AI to contribute across that work while the architect
remains responsible for the final claim. Advances in models, data, and tools
make parts of the approach practical now.

To apply that ambition to the Lighthouse prompt, we first need to say what
*architecture* and *architect* mean here. Architecture includes more than a
microarchitecture, block diagram, or chip artifact.

> **Architecture.** Architecture is the hardware-software contract and system
> organization that turn workload intent and technology constraints into a
> defensible system design. It includes instruction set architecture (ISA) and microarchitecture, memory and
> interconnect, accelerators and chiplets, compiler/runtime interfaces,
> physical-design constraints, verification, deployment, and the evidence used
> to justify choices.

> **Architect.** The architect is responsible for the decision. An architect
> frames the problem, chooses the abstractions, sets the evidence standard,
> defines what can reject a candidate, and remains answerable for the committed
> design. One person may hold this role, or several people may divide the work.
> The division of work must still state who may make each decision and who gives
> final approval. Methods can contribute, but the architect remains responsible
> for combining the results into a recommendation and defending the
> architectural claim.

## Architecture Questions in the Lighthouse prompt {#sec-why-the-prompt-spans-the-stack}

Because the architect remains responsible for defending the final claim, they must first unpack the prompt into specific, verifiable architectural decisions. @fig-moonshot-prompt makes the scale of the Lighthouse prompt explicit. The
prompt occupies only a few lines, but answering it requires choices about the
workload, software contract, compute organization, memory, power, compiler and
runtime, physical design, reliability, and verification. The bottom panel
shows the work required to connect those choices to tools, evidence, and an
architectural decision.

![**From design prompt to architecture study:** The prompt implies architecture requirements and engineering work that must be completed before an architect can formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend an answer.](images/F1-moonshot-stack){#fig-moonshot-prompt width="85%" fig-alt="Stacked diagram with the full Lighthouse prompt at the top, architecture requirements in the middle, and the execution and analysis requirements needed to support an answer at the bottom."}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

Start with the prompt itself. High-level intent first becomes questions and coupled design choices, not a generated chip artifact. The prompt asks the architect to compare several compute organizations
for a real-time mobile extended-reality (XR) workload while respecting a 3 W
thermal-design-power (TDP) budget and the limits of a low-power (LP) process.
Its individual phrases bound different parts of the study. "64-bit RISC-V-based
compute subsystem" establishes the ISA, ABI, and software contract.
"XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload" sets the workload, while
"Tensor Core accelerator block" leaves several compute
organizations open for comparison. The 3 W target and 3 nm-class LP process
supply physical limits that can rule out a proposal.

The prompt asks for a design-space report, but the study may also produce
software, models, RTL, layouts, prototypes, or a chip. How far implementation
must go depends on the claim. The report connects those artifacts and
measurements to the proposed design and explains why alternatives were
rejected. The prompt deliberately leaves the accelerator microarchitecture,
memory system, software integration, and evaluation tools open.

Progress toward the moonshot will require more than isolated demonstrations.
SPEC-style benchmarks made performance claims more comparable by specifying
workloads, run rules, and reporting standards. Shared simulators such as gem5
made architecture experiments easier to inspect across labs. FPGA-accelerated
simulation platforms such as FireSim, community benchmark suites such as
MLPerf, artifact-evaluation practices, and open tool flows have mattered
because they let other researchers understand, rerun, challenge, and safely
build on reported results [@SPEC2017CPU; @BinkertEtAl2011Gem5;
@KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim; @MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. The same principle applies
here. Shared tasks, benchmarks, tool interfaces, evaluation methods, and
evidence standards can make results useful beyond a single study.

@tbl-moonshot-decomposition collects the architectural decisions implied by
the Lighthouse prompt and the checks that could disqualify an answer. AI may
suggest a hardware and software decomposition. The architect accepts, revises,
or rejects that proposed scope and its coupled interfaces.

| **Prompt fragment** | **Architectural decisions** | **What must be checked** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **XRBench mobile XR** | Workload slice, input distribution, quality-of-service target, latency deadline, memory traffic, software pipeline, and drift assumptions. | Trace source, benchmark version, documented workload-phase selection, and rejection when results over-optimize a single hot loop or miss real-time behavior. |
| **64-bit RISC-V with vector or accelerator option** | ISA boundary, custom extension policy, programming model, compiler/runtime path, library support, and software compatibility. | Correctness, toolchain support, generated-code evidence, portability checks, and rejection of unsupported software semantics. |
| **Compute subsystem** | CPU, fixed-function accelerator, reconfigurable spatial array (e.g., CGRA or Reconfigurable Dataflow Architecture/RDA), or Processing-in-Memory (PIM) data-centric block; memory hierarchy, SoC integration, interface compatibility, and relevant reliability or isolation assumptions. | Design-space comparison and rejection of candidates that only win by moving cost, bandwidth, energy, complexity, or risk elsewhere. |
| **3 W, 3 nm-class low-power envelope** | Power, voltage/frequency, thermal, area, EDA constraints, and dynamic power management interfaces (e.g., Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling or DVFS, power domains, thermal sensors) for the OS/firmware. | Source, version, and calibration of the power model; physical-design limits (IR drop, electromigration, timing closure); sensitivity analysis; and rejection criteria for runtime throttling or reliability failures within the scoped workload. |
| **Design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives** | Alternatives, the Pareto frontier (the objective outcomes of feasible designs for which no alternative is at least as good on every objective and strictly better on at least one) [@Deb2001MultiObjective], assumptions, uncertainty, verification plan, rejected or failed candidates, and points at which the responsible architect decides whether to continue. | Measurements linked to assumptions and checks, rejected candidates, coverage, reproducible artifacts, and a named person responsible for the recommendation. |

: **What each prompt fragment requires:** The Lighthouse prompt maps to decisions about workload definition, software and ISA contracts, hardware organization, physical feasibility, and evidence. {#tbl-moonshot-decomposition tbl-colwidths="[18,34,35]"}

The table is a first-pass checklist rather than the final review form. An
answer is incomplete if it cannot identify its workload slice, software
contract, physical assumptions, supporting observations, or decision owner.
The rest of the book develops these requirements and turns them into a compact
review form.



Each row points to engineering that the short prompt leaves unstated. To judge
whether a candidate really fits the "3 W, 3 nm-class" constraint, architects
need results from RTL, synthesis, floorplanning, timing, IR drop, leakage,
thermal analysis, and signoff. Comparing the "RISC-V with vector or
accelerator option" alternatives also means checking the compiler, runtime,
libraries, generated code, and portability.
The prompt is therefore a computer architecture problem rather than a prompt
engineering exercise. A supported answer must connect decisions and evidence
across the stack.

The workload and process phrases also need more detail than the compact prompt
can provide. XRBench, a benchmark suite for mobile XR workloads, supplies a
workload anchor rather than a vague application label [@KwonEtAl2023XRBench].
End-to-end XR research shows how tightly the stages from perception to display
are coupled. Mobile XR is therefore a system-level architecture target, not a
single kernel to optimize [@HuzaifaEtAl2021ILLIXR]. AI may analyze dataset or
trace distributions to find missing phases and coverage gaps, complementing
profiling, trace analysis, and expert workload judgment. The architect remains
responsible for deciding whether the selected benchmark and distribution
represent the system claim. The process node is stated
as a class rather than as a named foundry process design kit (PDK). Current
mobile SoCs are publicly described in 3 nm-class technology, but the architect
must still name the process, libraries, voltage assumptions, and signoff path
[@Apple2024A18Pro; @Apple2024M4].

The architect might begin with an XRBench trace slice and software pipeline,
propose vector-width, local-memory, or CPU/accelerator alternatives, and screen
them with a cheap proxy. The architect can then reserve scarce compiler,
simulator, RTL, or physical-design feedback for the candidates whose results
could change the decision. That selection rule should be stated before the run,
along with what the resulting evidence may support.

Within that workflow, generation might produce legal vector configurations,
prediction might estimate their data-movement and power risks, and optimization
might choose which configurations merit expensive evaluation. A proxy may rank
the candidates one way, a simulator may overturn that ranking, and an EDA run
may return timing paths, congestion, or rule violations rather than a single
score. Search can also overfit[^fn-ml-overfitting] a convenient proxy.

Those methods can narrow the work, but their output alone does not show
whether a compiler assumption holds, whether the measured behavior follows
from the proposed mechanism, or whether the remaining tradeoff justifies a
commitment. The evidence should include the candidates, assumptions,
measurements, and rejections that affect the claim. It does not need every
internal model trace. The architect must resolve the remaining questions using
the complete body of evidence.

[^fn-ml-overfitting]: Overfitting, in machine learning, is fitting the noise and idiosyncrasies of a training sample so that performance fails to generalize to new data. An optimizer can overfit a proxy the same way, raising the proxy's measured score by exploiting its quirks rather than improving the true objective. Architects should therefore test a promising candidate with stronger evidence before committing to it.

## Efficiency Claims Across the Design Loop {#sec-efficiency-claims-need-rejectable-loops}

Even if an AI-assisted process can produce a candidate and report end to end, the efficiency claim still requires a scoped system comparison. The difference between a plausible candidate and a defensible design is
especially clear in an efficiency claim. The Lighthouse subsystem cannot be
judged from one score. Workload behavior, software support, data movement,
physical limits, verification effort, and deployment conditions can all change
which design is preferable. AI may help architects coordinate those choices,
but the resulting claim still needs evidence for the stated workload and
constraints.

Efficiency is a claim about how well a particular architecture performs useful
work with limited resources. The claim must name the design, workload, and
resource budget it covers. A faster design has not solved the problem if it
exceeds the power budget, cannot be verified, or depends on fragile software
assumptions.

Efficiency now spans more than performance and power. Classical computer
architecture made performance quantitative while also treating cost and power
as first-class constraints
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. Dennard-style scaling once
made it easier to improve performance while keeping power density manageable
[@DennardEtAl1974Scaling]. When that scaling regime weakened, dark silicon (the
share of a chip that must stay powered off to fit its thermal budget) and the
limits of multicore scaling pushed the field toward specialization
[@BorkarChien2011FutureMicroprocessors; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon; @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge].
The energy cost of data movement made arithmetic alone an incomplete measure
[@Horowitz2014Energy]. Warehouse-scale operation expanded the boundary to
power delivery, utilization, networking, operations, and total cost of
ownership [@BarrosoHolzleRanganathan2019DatacenterAsComputer].
Sustainability adds operational and embodied carbon to the comparison. These
depend on energy use, hardware manufacturing and infrastructure, utilization,
geography, and system lifetime [@GuptaEtAl2021ChasingCarbon].
An AI-assisted comparison must state the assumptions and evidence relevant to
each efficiency dimension. The architect should also name the violations that
would rule out a candidate.

A valid comparison must also account for failed and rejected alternatives, not
only the winners. Keeping those results prevents later architects from
reevaluating candidates that earlier evidence ruled out. It also shows
which parts of the design space the claim actually covers.

Modern benchmark suites already reflect this broader view. MLPerf, a community
effort to make machine-learning performance claims reproducible across
systems, includes deployment scenario, latency, throughput, and accuracy in its
comparisons [@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. Useful work must be evaluated
against the resource that is scarce in the stated setting, whether that is
latency, throughput, energy, power, area, cost, carbon, reliability,
verification effort, engineering time, or risk.

The design, workload, scenario, and scarce resource together define what
"efficient" means. For the Lighthouse system, a candidate that improves average
throughput may still be a poor choice if it misses an XR frame deadline, exceeds
the 3 W envelope under sustained load, or adds verification work that the
project cannot absorb. Changing any of those conditions changes the claim and
the evidence needed to support it.

@tbl-efficiency-dimensions separates six dimensions that an architect may need
to compare. The architect should select the dimensions that define the claim.
Reading the rows together also shows where a gain may shift cost or risk
elsewhere.

| **Dimension** | **Efficiency question** | **Why it complicates the comparison** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Performance** | How much useful work is delivered per unit time, latency budget, or service-level target? | The answer depends on workload selection, scenario, software stack, and whether the measured behavior matches the deployment claim. |
| **Power and energy** | How much useful work is delivered per watt, joule, thermal budget, or battery envelope? | A credible comparison must account for activity, data movement, voltage/frequency choices, thermal constraints, and fidelity gaps between estimates and signoff. |
| **Reliability and correctness** | How much useful work survives faults, corner cases, nondeterminism, and validation? | A faster candidate is not efficient if it spends its savings on fragility, debug burden, or invalid software and hardware assumptions. |
| **Scalability and cost** | How much useful work is delivered per dollar, rack, network hop, operator action, or unit of capacity? | Local wins can shift cost to memory, network, power delivery, utilization, operations, or total cost of ownership. |
| **Sustainability** | How much useful work is delivered per unit of operational and embodied environmental footprint? | Carbon depends on hardware lifetime, manufacturing, energy mix, utilization, and where and when computation runs. |
| **Evidence and engineering effort** | How much decision-relevant evidence is obtained per simulation, experiment, verification run, or engineer-hour? | Generating more candidates can still be inefficient if evaluation consumes scarce feedback, hides failures, or cannot reject weak outputs. |

: **Dimensions of an efficiency claim:** Performance, power, reliability, scalability, sustainability, and evidence cost are increasingly coupled. A valid comparison must show when an apparent improvement shifts cost or changes the scope. {#tbl-efficiency-dimensions tbl-colwidths="[22,32,34]"}

Two kinds of efficiency are present in this comparison. The proposed system
must perform useful work within its resource limits. The study must also use
simulator runs, physical-design experiments, verification effort, and expert
review carefully enough to reach a reliable conclusion. Reducing the cost of
the study is valuable only when the remaining observations can still distinguish
the alternatives and expose the failures that matter to the decision.

These dimensions interact. In the mobile XR setting anchored by XRBench and ILLIXR, frame latency, energy, sustained power, and data movement are tightly coupled; a gain in one can easily violate a deadline or thermal limit in another. Better simulated performance can also be offset by added
verification work. Lower energy may depend on fragile software assumptions,
and a candidate that wins under a proxy may fail on a more faithful workload.
The architect must explain which tradeoffs are acceptable for the stated
workload and constraints. An efficiency claim must state its scope instead of
relying on one attractive artifact or score.

These coupled requirements do not mean every architecture project needs AI.
Traditional architecture methods remain effective when the workload,
abstraction, feedback path, and proposed change are clear. Architects should
first identify what familiar models, scripts, simulation, and expert review
already handle well.
They can then state what they expect AI to improve. The test is whether the
assistance leads to a better system or a more reliable conclusion, not simply
more artifacts.

The Lighthouse prompt makes this concrete. The requested subsystem must
support a workload class, meet a power envelope, fit a technology assumption,
interact with software, and produce evidence. The comparison must weigh
energy, latency, memory traffic, programmability, verification, and deployment
risk. A single scalar objective may help an automated search, but it cannot be
the whole architectural judgment.

```{=latex}
\Needspace{20\baselineskip}
```

## Existing Systems Cover Parts of the Design Loop

Current work has demonstrated useful parts of AI-assisted architecture under
bounded conditions. Predictive design-space exploration uses simulated performance to train surrogate models that estimate behavior across a specified design space [@IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE]. AutoTVM uses measured hardware execution to guide a statistical search for tensor-program schedules [@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM]. In each case, the input, architecture job, feedback source, and bounded result remain clear. We use a cache subsystem throughout this text as a running example of how an architect might leverage these tools to rapidly evaluate varying replacement policies or memory hierarchies.

AlphaChip is a prominent physical-design example. Its inputs include a netlist and macro descriptions; its job is macro placement; its feedback comes from proxy rewards like wirelength and congestion. The original paper reported
macro placements produced in under six hours, and later first-party reports
said the method contributed to several TPU generations
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @GoldieEtAl2024AlphaChipAddendum;
@GoogleDeepMind2024AlphaChip]. @sec-proxy-mismatch-metric-gaming-and-calibration
examines the public disagreement about its comparisons and proxy evidence.

While these tools succeed at their individual tasks, they also expose the integration problem required to complete a whole architecture study. To move from isolated tool successes to an end-to-end design loop, the architecture community must learn from adjacent fields. The ML and science communities accelerated their progress by adopting shared baselines, standardized datasets, and rigorous artifact evaluation. For example, ImageNet drove the deep learning revolution by providing a common benchmark, while CASP (Critical Assessment of Structure Prediction) allowed AlphaFold's capabilities to be systematically measured against objective, community-held standards. These happened in ML and structural biology, so we must adopt them in computer architecture. Shared tasks, public datasets, and open evaluation protocols will be essential to combine narrow tool use into a robust, reviewable AI-assisted architecture practice.

## Open Problems in AI-Assisted Architecture

The complete Lighthouse process remains a research direction. Architectural knowledge, tool execution, role composition, and evidence remain separate engineering problems. Before addressing these specific capabilities, @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales explores why the traditional architecture design loop is struggling to scale, and @sec-architecture-20-ontology provides the framework for decomposing the architecture task. We avoid being too specific about tools because they change, focusing on fundamentals. Later chapters take up the remaining requirements:

- @sec-data-representations-world-models explains architectural knowledge, current design state, and predicted consequences.
- @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces explains legal multi-tool action and typed feedback.
- @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization explains AI roles and component organization.
- @sec-feedback-verification-trust explains evidence, challenge, and decision relevance.
- @sec-running-the-loop shows one bounded integrated study.
- @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack shows what changes across feedback conditions and time.
- @sec-what-architect-owns synthesizes architect responsibility.

Models, tools, and benchmarks will continue to change. The durable questions
concern the architecture decision each system advances, the work AI performs,
the observations that can challenge it, and the limits of the resulting claim.
Detailed installation instructions remain in
@sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping, while @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card provides a
compact way to inspect a completed study.

Architectural responsibility remains assigned throughout this research
program. Architects frame the task, choose abstractions and fidelity, decide
what evidence is adequate, interpret the mechanisms, and determine what may be
automated. AI can change how much of the work is delegated without deciding by
itself what the resulting evidence supports.

## Open Research Questions

The Lighthouse prompt and the bounded systems discussed in this chapter leave
five questions unresolved. Progress will require better representations, tool
interfaces, methods, evidence, and decision practices.

1. **When does AI-assisted design work become an architecture contribution?**
An AI method may be novel without improving what architects can understand, build, compare, or decide. Another method may improve an architecture decision under hardware/software and physical constraints without introducing a novel AI technique. Reviewers must distinguish those cases. An architecture contribution can include improved formulation or decomposition, not only a candidate artifact. The work must advance an artifact, method, dataset, environment, finding, or decision that supports an architectural claim.

2. **How can we tell whether AI helped the architect understand the design?**
A candidate score does not show whether AI exposed a missing cross-layer dependency,
changed the next experiment, connected measurements to mechanisms, or
recognized why an apparent improvement will not transfer. The hard part is
distinguishing an explanation that changes what the architect understands or
does next from a fluent summary written after the result is known. Useful assistance
must reveal a mechanism or support a decision rather than repeat an expected
explanation. This is Hamming's distinction between producing insight and
producing more numbers.

3. **What evidence is sufficient for an AI-assisted claim when complete reproduction is impossible?**
Architecture results often depend on proprietary workloads, process design
kits, training inputs, tool settings, or deployed systems that outside groups
cannot inspect. Reviewers must decide which combination of public artifacts,
matched baselines, sensitivity studies, independent checks, and limited-access
review is enough to understand what AI contributed and how broadly the
architectural claim applies. AlphaChip makes this problem concrete, but the
same difficulty appears throughout industrial architecture research. Any
answer must distinguish a credible deployment report, a reproducible public
comparison, and a claim that remains unresolved.

4. **How can reviewers identify unsupported decisions in AI-assisted architecture work?**
Architecture reviewers already know how to inspect artifacts, baselines, and
quantitative tradeoffs. AI participation adds questions about the inputs and
artifact versions available to a method, the actions it was allowed to take,
the alternatives it rejected, and the decisions reserved for people. Reviewers
need enough of this information to reconstruct why a candidate advanced or
failed and how the work reached a decision. They do not need every internal
model trace, and proprietary tools may make full inspection impossible. But the
inputs, actions, results, and human judgments that affected the decision must
still be available for review.

5. **What should architects share so AI-assisted results can be compared and built on?**
If AI-assisted architecture remains a collection of isolated demonstrations like the bounded systems discussed earlier,
architects will struggle to compare results. A common basis could include
shared problem definitions and case studies, workload and tool environments,
baselines and experiment protocols, and records of rejected alternatives.
Independent groups need enough of this infrastructure to compare methods
fairly, learn from negative results, and build on a finding after the original
demonstration. It should support those uses without forcing every architecture
problem into one fixed benchmark.

## Conclusion

The Architecture 2.0 moonshot is not about replacing the architect; it is about elevating the architect's capacity to navigate complex, multi-dimensional design spaces. The Lighthouse prompt illustrates that moving from system intent to a defensible system design requires far more than generating a plausible artifact. It demands a rigorous process that integrates performance, energy, reliability, and cost within strict physical and software constraints.

While current point solutions like surrogate models or AlphaChip demonstrate what is possible in narrow domains, the true challenge lies in end-to-end integration. To achieve this, the architecture community must embrace the rigorous evaluation standards and shared baselines that accelerated progress in the ML and scientific communities.

Ultimately, faster proposal generation only matters if we have the evidence and insight to distinguish good ideas from dangerous failures. The tools and models will inevitably evolve, but the core responsibility remains unchanged: the architect must frame the problem, demand robust evidence, and remain accountable for the final design. The following chapters will provide the foundational framework to make this AI-assisted workflow a reality.

As you move forward into the rest of the book, keep these core principles in mind. They form the basis for evaluating any new AI-assisted design technique.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Evaluate AI across the complete architecture work it claims to assist. A generated artifact matters only in relation to the question, alternatives, implementation path, evaluation, explanation, and decision it advances.
- **Reader test:** Can a reader take the lighthouse request, identify its workload, software, hardware, memory, physical, verification, and decision questions, and state which parts current AI systems have and have not demonstrated?
- **Up next:** @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales explains why the architecture work introduced here has become harder to complete as specialization, software change, evaluation cost, and cross-layer coordination reinforce one another.
:::
